## Entry 45
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:11:50.275Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:11:50.275Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_k9B2dFBc2nl5hK5l0ZLQ8ROR",
    "output": "Chunk ID: 31fa6c\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1473\nOutput:\n3:### Version 4.0 — Normalized Schema, Agentic RAG, No Vector DB, 12 Tables Final\n39:- Normalized Postgres schema — raw sensor/telemetry data only, no derived or computed fields in DB\n93:The system recognizes Bangalore's major BBMP wards as canonical zones. A `location_master` table with canonical names, aliases, and geometry is the grounding layer. This prevents the LLM from misinterpreting freeform location references.\n134:The Postgres schema is not a replica of any single real-world database. In reality BESCOM stores outage logs in a proprietary OMS, BBMP drainage data exists in scattered Excel files and physical maps, KSNDMC lake levels are published as PDFs and HTML tables, and IMD data comes as fixed-width text files.\n136:The Postgres schema is a **designed consolidation layer** — real-world sources are cleaned, transformed, and loaded into this schema. For development the data is mocked to mirror realistic patterns. The developer will find real datasets from IMD, CPCB OpenAQ, KSNDMC, BESCOM annual reports, Uber Movement, and TomTom Move, then mirror them into the mock database.\n254:  • Resolves location to ward_id via location_master\n285:**Long query handling:** Complex compound queries can take 5-10 seconds. Since there is no SSE the Frontend shows a loading state. This is acceptable for internal government use. Users are not consumer users expecting instant responses.\n300:  get_lake_hydrology(lake_id)\n301:  get_flood_incidents_historical(location_id)\n310:- `fill_percentage` + `overflow_status` + `surplus_flow_cusecs` from `lake_hydrology` drives backflow risk\n386:  get_power_outage_events(location_id, window_days)\n398:- `fault_type = treefall` outages in `power_outage_event` are the direct calibration signal\n408:    \"power_outage_probability\": {},\n436:- `rainfall_mm_1h` + `corridor_name` + `time_band` + `weekday_weekend_flag` from `traffic_segment_observation` maps to delay factor reasoning\n511:      \"table\": \"lake_hydrology\",\n544:      \"power_outage_risk\": 0.62,\n644:Documents in this system are domain-specific and well-structured. IMD bulletins have standard formats. Drainage plans have numbered sections. Flood post-mortems have clear chapter structures. Index-based agentic navigation is more deterministic and auditable than embedding-based similarity search for this use case. The agent reads the document index, reasons about which section contains the answer, then fetches only that section. No embeddings, no similarity scores, no black-box retrieval.\n652:- Static entity properties separated into reference tables\n653:- No document-derived tables — handled by Agentic RAG pipeline\n654:- No precomputed/derived tables — handled by pipeline or computed at query time by agents\n656:**Final table count: 12**\n662:**1. location_master** — canonical zone/ward/colony registry, spine of the DB\n747:**5. air_quality_observation** — raw readings from CPCB monitoring stations\n776:**6. lake_hydrology** — live telemetry readings per lake per timestamp\n805:**7. power_outage_event** — historical outage log per incident\n836:**8. traffic_segment_observation** — live and historical congestion per road segment\n947:12 tables total. Every table maps directly to a use case or operational necessity. No derived fields, no computed scores, no document metadata — those are handled by the Agentic RAG pipeline outside Postgres.\n951:| 1 | location_master | Reference | All agents — ward, colony, zone resolution |\n955:| 5 | air_quality_observation | Sensor | Heat and health agent |\n956:| 6 | lake_hydrology | Sensor | Flood agent — live lake telemetry |\n957:| 7 | power_outage_event | Log | Infrastructure stress agent |\n958:| 8 | traffic_segment_observation | Sensor | Logistics disruption agent |\n987:Cons: arbitrary chunking breaks document context, retrieval is a black box — hard to audit why a chunk was returned, embedding model adds infrastructure overhead, accuracy depends on chunk quality which is hard to control for government PDFs that have tables, diagrams, and non-standard formatting.\n999:This approach is known as **PageIndex** — the agent reasons over structure first, navigates like a human expert reading a report, then fetches only the section it needs. No vector DB, no embeddings, fully auditable, every retrieval step is traceable.\n1004:- Retrieval must be auditable — a BBMP officer asking \"why did the system recommend barricading?\" must be able to trace the answer to an exact section and page number\n1048:  \"table_of_contents\": [\n1121:    # Agent uses the table_of_contents to decide which section to fetch\n1134:2. Agent reads table_of_contents\n1347:│  • PostgreSQL connection pool — all 12 structured tables             │\n1481:│   │   │   │   └── pg_tools.py          # lake_hydrology, flood_incident queries\n1482:│   │   │   └── schemas.py               # FloodRiskPayload Pydantic model\n1487:│   │   │   │   └── pg_tools.py          # air_quality_observation, ward_profile queries\n1488:│   │   │   └── schemas.py               # HeatHealthPayload Pydantic model\n1493:│   │   │   │   └── pg_tools.py          # power_outage_event queries\n1494:│   │   │   └── schemas.py               # InfraPayload Pydantic model\n1499:│   │   │   │   └── pg_tools.py          # traffic_segment_observation queries\n1500:│   │   │   └── schemas.py               # LogisticsPayload Pydantic model\n1513:│   └── schemas/\n1536:│       ├── seed_location_master.sql     # Bangalore wards + colonies with aliases\n1541:│       ├── seed_lake_hydrology.sql      # Mock lake telemetry\n1544:│       └── seed_flood_incidents.sql     # 2022 flood incident data — critical for evals\n1638:- How Postgres rows are formatted before injection — JSON, table, or natural language\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 49
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:11:53.687Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:11:53.687Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_04mYtTrxuk25b9tGdqxBuwQB",
    "output": "Chunk ID: a99c8e\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1929\nOutput:\n| Document section text | File storage | Full text per section referenced by index |\n\n### No Vector Database\n\nDocuments in this system are domain-specific and well-structured. IMD bulletins have standard formats. Drainage plans have numbered sections. Flood post-mortems have clear chapter structures. Index-based agentic navigation is more deterministic and auditable than embedding-based similarity search for this use case. The agent reads the document index, reasons about which section contains the answer, then fetches only that section. No embeddings, no similarity scores, no black-box retrieval.\n\n---\n\n## 8. PostgreSQL Schema — Final Normalized Tables\n\n**Design rules applied:**\n- Raw sensor and reported facts only — no derived or computed fields\n- Static entity properties separated into reference tables\n- No document-derived tables — handled by Agentic RAG pipeline\n- No precomputed/derived tables — handled by pipeline or computed at query time by agents\n\n**Final table count: 12**\n\n---\n\n### Reference Tables\n\n**1. location_master** — canonical zone/ward/colony registry, spine of the DB\n```\nlocation_id\ncanonical_name\nlocation_type          -- ward / colony / neighborhood / lake / road / underpass\naliases                -- array of alternate names\nward_id\nward_name\nzone_name              -- North / South / East / West / Central\nneighborhood_name\npin_code\nlatitude\nlongitude\ngeometry\ncreated_at\nupdated_at\n```\n\n**2. ward_profile** — static demographic and land character data per ward\n```\nprofile_id\nward_id\npopulation_density\nelderly_population_pct\nchildren_population_pct\nslum_household_count\ngreen_cover_pct\nimpervious_surface_pct\ndata_year              -- census/survey year this data came from\nsource_name\n```\n\n**3. lake_reference** — static physical properties of each lake\n```\nlake_id\nlake_name\nlake_type              -- natural / tank / reservoir\nlocation_id\nward_id\nlatitude\nlongitude\ncatchment_area_sqkm\nlake_area_sqkm\nfull_tank_level_meters\nmax_capacity_mcm\nnumber_of_gates\nsource_name\n```\n\n---\n\n### Sensor / Live Telemetry Tables\n\n**4. weather_observation** — raw readings from AWS/ARG/manual stations\n```\nobservation_id\nobserved_at\ningested_at\nstation_id\nstation_name\nstation_type           -- AWS / ARG / Manual\nsource_name\nlocation_id\nward_id\nlatitude\nlongitude\nelevation_meters\n\ntemperature_celsius\nhumidity_percent\npressure_hpa\n\nwind_speed_kmh\nwind_gust_kmh\nwind_direction_degrees\n\nrainfall_mm_hourly\nrainfall_mm_24hour\nrainfall_intensity_mm_hr\n\nvisibility_km\n\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n**5. air_quality_observation** — raw readings from CPCB monitoring stations\n```\naq_obs_id\nobserved_at\ningested_at\nstation_id\nstation_name\nstation_type           -- continuous / manual\nsource_name\nlocation_id\nward_id\nlatitude\nlongitude\n\npm2_5\npm10\nno2\nso2\nco\nozone\nnh3\n\naqi_value\naqi_category           -- Good / Satisfactory / Moderate / Poor / Very Poor / Severe\ndominant_pollutant\n\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n**6. lake_hydrology** — live telemetry readings per lake per timestamp\n```\nlake_obs_id\nobserved_at\ningested_at\nlake_id                -- FK to lake_reference\nsource_name\n\nwater_level_meters\nfill_percentage\n\ninflow_cusecs\noutflow_cusecs\nsurplus_flow_cusecs\n\nrainfall_last_1h_mm\nrainfall_last_24h_mm\n\noverflow_status\ngate_status            -- open / closed / partial\nnumber_of_gates_open\nalert_level            -- Normal / Watch / Warning / Danger\n\nencroachment_risk_flag\nsiltation_risk_flag\n\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n**7. power_outage_event** — historical outage log per incident\n```\noutage_id\nevent_id\nstarted_at\nrestored_at\nduration_minutes\nlocation_id\nward_id\nzone_name\n\nfeeder_id\nfeeder_name\nsubstation_id\nsubstation_name\n\noutage_type            -- planned / unplanned\nfault_type             -- treefall / conductor / transformer / lightning / overload\ncause_text\naffected_customers\ncritical_load_affected_flag\n\nwind_speed_kmh_at_time\nwind_gust_kmh_at_time\nrainfall_mm_1h_at_time\nrainfall_mm_24h_at_time\n\nsource_name\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n**8. traffic_segment_observation** — live and historical congestion per road segment\n```\ntraffic_obs_id\nobserved_at\ningested_at\nroad_segment_id\nroad_name\ncorridor_name          -- ORR / Sarjapur Road / Hosur Road / Bellary Road\nlocation_id\nward_id\nlatitude_start\nlongitude_start\nlatitude_end\nlongitude_end\n\nroad_class             -- highway / arterial / local\nlanes_count\nspeed_limit_kmh\n\navg_speed_kmh\nfree_flow_speed_kmh\ntravel_time_minutes\nfree_flow_travel_time_minutes\ndelay_minutes\ncongestion_index\n\nheavy_vehicle_share\nwaterlogging_flag\nincident_type\nincident_severity\n\nrainfall_mm_1h_at_time\nrainfall_mm_3h_at_time\n\nsource_name\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n**9. flood_incident** — reported flood and waterlogging incidents\n```\nincident_id\nreported_at\nresolved_at\nlocation_id\nward_id\nneighborhood_name\nlatitude\nlongitude\n\nwater_depth_cm\nroad_blocked_flag\nvehicles_stranded_flag\nproperty_damage_flag\npump_deployed_flag\n\nsource_type            -- bbmp_log / field_report / news / citizen_report\nseverity               -- low / medium / high / critical\nrainfall_mm_1h_at_time\nrainfall_mm_3h_at_time\n\ndata_quality_flag\n```\n\n---\n\n### Backend Operational Tables\n\n**10. sessions**\n```\nsession_id\nuser_role              -- bbmp_internal / planner / logistics\ncreated_at\nlast_active_at\ntotal_turns\nactive_flag\n```\n\n**11. conversation_history**\n```\nturn_id\nsession_id\nrole                   -- user / assistant\nmessage\nagents_invoked\nresponse_mode          -- text / scorecard / chart\ncited_documents\ncited_records\ntimestamp\nlatency_ms\n```\n\n**12. audit_log**\n```\nlog_id\nsession_id\nturn_id\nquery_text\nintent_classified\nagents_invoked\noverall_confidence\ndata_freshness_lag_seconds\nstale_sources\nerror_flag\nerror_detail\ncreated_at\n```\n\n---\n\n### Table Summary\n\n12 tables total. Every table maps directly to a use case or operational necessity. No derived fields, no computed scores, no document metadata — those are handled by the Agentic RAG pipeline outside Postgres.\n\n| # | Table | Type | Serves |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | location_master | Reference | All agents — ward, colony, zone resolution |\n| 2 | ward_profile | Reference | Heat agent — population vulnerability scoring |\n| 3 | lake_reference | Reference | Flood agent — static lake physical properties |\n| 4 | weather_observation | Sensor | Flood, heat, infra, logistics agents |\n| 5 | air_quality_observation | Sensor | Heat and health agent |\n| 6 | lake_hydrology | Sensor | Flood agent — live lake telemetry |\n| 7 | power_outage_event | Log | Infrastructure stress agent |\n| 8 | traffic_segment_observation | Sensor | Logistics disruption agent |\n| 9 | flood_incident | Log | Flood agent — historical incident record |\n| 10 | sessions | Operational | Backend — session lifecycle management |\n| 11 | conversation_history | Operational | Chat agent — memory and turn history |\n| 12 | audit_log | Operational | Audit trail, eval fixtures, debugging |\n\n**What is not in Postgres and why:**\n\n| Removed | Reason |\n|---|---|\n| document_catalog | Replaced by JSON index files in Agentic RAG pipeline |\n| drainage_asset, underpass_risk, grid_asset_risk, tree_canopy_zone | Document-derived — extracted by pipeline, stored as structured JSON sections |\n| neighborhood_risk_feature, logistics_weather_delay_model, climate_baseline | Precomputed derived — pipeline responsibility, not raw sensor data |\n| historical_city_event | Derived from documents — lives in RAG pipeline output |\n| asset_relationship | Linked document-derived assets — no longer needed |\n\n---\n\n## 9. Agentic RAG — Document Intelligence Layer\n\n### Why No Vector Database\n\nThree approaches were evaluated. The decision is to use Agentic RAG with structured JSON indexing — no vector DB.\n"
  }
}
```

No derived fields, no computed scores, no document metadata — those are handled by the Agentic RAG pipeline outside Postgres.\n\n| # | Table | Type | Serves |\n|---|---|---|---|\n| 1 | location_master | Reference | All agents — ward, colony, zone resolution |\n| 2 | ward_profile | Reference | Heat agent — population vulnerability scoring |\n| 3 | lake_reference | Reference | Flood agent — static lake physical properties |\n| 4 | weather_observation | Sensor | Flood, heat, infra, logistics agents |\n| 5 | air_quality_observation | Sensor | Heat and health agent |\n| 6 | lake_hydrology | Sensor | Flood agent — live lake telemetry |\n| 7 | power_outage_event | Log | Infrastructure stress agent |\n| 8 | traffic_segment_observation | Sensor | Logistics disruption agent |\n| 9 | flood_incident | Log | Flood agent — historical incident record |\n| 10 | sessions | Operational | Backend — session lifecycle management |\n| 11 | conversation_history | Operational | Chat agent — memory and turn history |\n| 12 | audit_log | Operational | Audit trail, eval fixtures, debugging |\n\n**What is not in Postgres and why:**\n\n| Removed | Reason |\n|---|---|\n| document_catalog | Replaced by JSON index files in Agentic RAG pipeline |\n| drainage_asset, underpass_risk, grid_asset_risk, tree_canopy_zone | Document-derived — extracted by pipeline, stored as structured JSON sections |\n| neighborhood_risk_feature, logistics_weather_delay_model, climate_baseline | Precomputed derived — pipeline responsibility, not raw sensor data |\n| historical_city_event | Derived from documents — lives in RAG pipeline output |\n| asset_relationship | Linked document-derived assets — no longer needed |\n\n---\n\n## 9. Agentic RAG — Document Intelligence Layer\n\n### Why No Vector Database\n\nThree approaches were evaluated. The decision is to use Agentic RAG with structured JSON indexing — no vector DB.\n"
```json
  }
}
```
---

APIRouter, Depends, status\nfrom pydantic import BaseModel, ConfigDict\nfrom starlette.responses import JSONResponse\n\nfrom app.api.deps import settings\nfrom app.core.config import Settings\nfrom app.services import agent_proxy\n\nrouter = APIRouter(prefix=\"/api\", tags=[\"chat\"])\n\n\nclass ArtifactPayload(BaseModel):\n    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"ignore\")\n\n    type: str\n    title: str\n    source: str\n    description: str | None = None\n\n\nclass RiskMetric(BaseModel):\n    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"ignore\")\n\n    probability: float | None = None\n    severity: str | None = None\n    congestion_score: float | None = None\n\n\nclass HealthAdvisory(BaseModel):\n    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"ignore\")\n\n    aqi: int | None = None\n    aqi_category: str | None = None\n\n\nclass EmergencyReadiness(BaseModel):\n    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"ignore\")\n\n    recommendation: str\n    actions: list[str] | None = None\n\n\nclass RiskCardPayload(BaseModel):\n    model_config = ConfigDict(extra=\"ignore\")\n\n    neighborhood: str\n    generated_at: str\n    overall_risk_score: float\n    flood_risk: RiskMetric | None = None\n    power_outage_risk: RiskMetric | None = None\n    traffic_delay_index: RiskMetric | None = None\n    health_advisory: HealthAdvisory | None = None\n    emergency_readiness: EmergencyReadiness | None = None\n\n\nclass ChatRequest(BaseModel):\n    session_id: str\n    message: str\n\n\nclass ChatResponse(BaseModel):\n    message: str\n    risk_card: RiskCardPayload | None = None\n    artifact: ArtifactPayload | None = None\n\n\nclass ErrorPayload(BaseModel):\n    code: str\n    message: str\n\n\nclass CompositeHealthResponse(BaseModel):\n    backend: str\n    agent: str\n    status: str\n\n\ndef _error_response(code: str, message: str, status_code: int) -> JSONResponse:\n    return JSONResponse(\n        status_code=status_code,\n        content={\"error\": ErrorPayload(code=code, message=message).model_dump()},\n    )\n\n\n@router.post(\n    \"/chat\",\n    response_model=ChatResponse,\n    responses={\n        502: {\"description\": \"Agent unavailable\"},\n        504: {\"description\": \"Agent timeout\"},\n    },\n)\nasync def chat(\n    payload: ChatRequest,\n    app_settings: Settings = Depends(settings),\n) -> ChatResponse | JSONResponse:\n    try:\n        response_payload = await agent_proxy.get_chat_response(\n            settings=app_settings,\n            session_id=payload.session_id,\n            message=payload.message,\n        )\n    except httpx.TimeoutException:\n        return _error_response(\n            code=\"AGENT_TIMEOUT\",\n            message=\"The agent service did not respond in time.\",\n            status_code=status.HTTP_504_GATEWAY_TIMEOUT,\n        )\n    except (httpx.HTTPError, ValueError):\n        return _error_response(\n            code=\"AGENT_UNAVAILABLE\",\n            message=\"The agent service is currently unavailable.\",\n            status_code=status.HTTP_502_BAD_GATEWAY,\n        )\n\n    return ChatResponse.model_validate(response_payload)\n\n\n@router.get(\"/health\", response_model=CompositeHealthResponse)\nasync def api_health(app_settings: Settings = Depends(settings)) -> CompositeHealthResponse:\n    try:\n        payload = await agent_proxy.get_agent_health(app_settings)\n    except httpx.HTTPError:\n        payload = {\"backend\": \"ok\", \"agent\": \"down\", \"status\": \"degraded\"}\n\n    return CompositeHealthResponse.model_validate(payload)\n\n\n@router.delete(\"/chat/{session_id}\", status_code=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT)\nasync def clear_chat_session(\n    session_id: str,  # noqa: ARG001 - placeholder until agents service supports reset\n    app_settings: Settings = Depends(settings),  # noqa: ARG001\n) -> None:\n    return None\n"
```json
  }
}
```

```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:12:00.844Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_91EoGo1Loy38H2bMs8I5Ixqw",
    "output": "Chunk ID: e7a319\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 797\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom typing import Any\n\nimport httpx\n\nfrom app.core.config import Settings\n\nCHAT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = 30.0\n\n\ndef _auth_headers(settings: Settings) -> dict[str, str]:\n    token = settings.agent_internal_token.strip()\n    if not token:\n        raise ValueError(\"AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN must be set for agent requests\")\n    return {\"X-Internal-Token\": token}\n\n\ndef _extract_message_from_events(events: list[dict[str, Any]]) -> str:\n    for event in reversed(events):\n        content = event.get(\"content\")\n        if not isinstance(content, dict):\n            continue\n        role = content.get(\"role\")\n        if role not in (None, \"model\"):\n            continue\n        parts = content.get(\"parts\")\n        if not isinstance(parts, list):\n            continue\n        texts = [\n            part.get(\"text\")\n            for part in parts\n            if isinstance(part, dict) and isinstance(part.get(\"text\"), str)\n        ]\n        if texts:\n            return \"\\n\".join(texts)\n    raise ValueError(\"Agent response did not include model text\")\n\n\nasync def _ensure_session(\n    client: httpx.AsyncClient,\n    base_url: str,\n    app_name: str,\n    user_id: str,\n    session_id: str,\n    headers: dict[str, str],\n) -> None:\n    response = await client.post(\n        f\"{base_url}/apps/{app_name}/users/{user_id}/sessions/{session_id}\",\n        headers=headers,\n        json=None,\n    )\n    if response.status_code in {200, 201, 204, 409}:\n        return\n    response.raise_for_status()\n\n\nasync def get_chat_response(settings: Settings, session_id: str, message: str) -> dict[str, Any]:\n    headers = _auth_headers(settings)\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=CHAT_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) as client:\n        base_url = settings.agent_server_url.rstrip(\"/\")\n        app_name = settings.agent_app_name\n        user_id = \"metrosense\"\n        await _ensure_session(\n            client=client,\n            base_url=base_url,\n            app_name=app_name,\n            user_id=user_id,\n            session_id=session_id,\n            headers=headers,\n        )\n        response = await client.post(\n            f\"{base_url}/run\",\n            headers=headers,\n            json={\n                \"appName\": app_name,\n                \"userId\": user_id,\n                \"sessionId\": session_id,\n                \"newMessage\": {\"role\": \"user\", \"parts\": [{\"text\": message}]},\n                \"streaming\": False,\n            },\n        )\n        response.raise_for_status()\n        events = response.json()\n\n    if not isinstance(events, list):\n        raise ValueError(\"Agent response must be a list of events\")\n\n    message_text = _extract_message_from_events(events)\n\n    return {\n        \"message\": message_text,\n        \"risk_card\": None,\n        \"artifact\": None,\n    }\n\n\nasync def get_agent_health(settings: Settings) -> dict[str, str]:\n    headers = _auth_headers(settings)\n    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=5.0) as client:\n        response = await client.get(\n            f\"{settings.agent_server_url.rstrip('/')}/health\",\n            headers=headers,\n        )\n        response.raise_for_status()\n\n    return {\"backend\": \"ok\", \"agent\": \"ok\", \"status\": \"online\"}\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 65
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:12:02.693Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:12:02.693Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call_output",
    "call_id": "call_62EnCjSKbXM6Z8Mk5bF7AbGu",
    "output": "Chunk ID: ee69f9\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 1381\nOutput:\n# MetroSense\n\nMetroSense is a chat-first climate and infrastructure intelligence system for Bengaluru, built with FastAPI, React, and PostgreSQL. This repo includes the backend, frontend, and internal agents service plus the tooling and quality gates needed to evolve the product.\n\n## Tech Stack\n\n| Layer | Technology |\n|-------|-----------|\n| Backend | FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 (async), Alembic, PostgreSQL 16 |\n| Frontend | React 18, TypeScript, Vite 5, Material-UI v6 |\n| Package Managers | uv (Python), pnpm (Node) |\n| Quality | Ruff, MyPy (strict), import-linter, ESLint, TypeScript strict |\n| Containers | Docker, Docker Compose |\n| CI | GitHub Actions |\n\n## Project Structure\n\n```\n.\n├── .github/workflows/ci.yml    # CI pipeline\n├── docker-compose.yml           # PostgreSQL + API + Client\n├── Dockerfile                   # Multi-stage production build\n├── server/                      # FastAPI backend\n│   ├── app/\n│   │   ├── api/routes/          # HTTP endpoints\n│   │   ├── core/                # Config + logging\n│   │   ├── db/                  # SQLAlchemy models + session\n│   │   ├── middleware/          # Error handler + request logging\n│   │   ├── services/            # Business logic layer\n│   │   └── main.py              # App factory\n│   ├── alembic/                 # Database migrations\n│   ├── tests/                   # Unit + integration tests\n│   └── pyproject.toml           # Dependencies + tool config\n├── agents/                      # ADK agent service (internal)\n│   ├── metrosearch_agent/        # Simple Google Search-enabled agent\n│   ├── scripts/                 # Smoke tests\n│   └── pyproject.toml           # Dependencies + tool config\n└── client/                      # React frontend\n    ├── src/\n    │   ├── App.tsx              # Router + pages\n    │   ├── main.tsx             # Entry point + providers\n    │   └── lib/api.ts           # Axios instance\n    ├── package.json             # Dependencies + scripts\n    └── Dockerfile               # Node build → Nginx\n```\n\n## Module Boundaries\n\n- `app.api` depends on `app.services`, `app.core` only\n- `app.services` depends on `app.db`, `app.core` only (must NOT import from `app.api`)\n- `app.db` depends on `app.core` only (must NOT import from `app.services` or `app.api`)\n- Enforced by `import-linter` in CI\n\n## Quick Start\n\nThe agents service is protected by a shared secret header in dev. The backend talks to an agents proxy on port 8020, and that proxy forwards to the ADK server on port 8021. Set the same `AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN` in both services so the proxy can validate requests.\n\n```bash\n# Start PostgreSQL\ndocker-compose up -d db\n\n# Backend\ncd server\nuv sync --all-extras\nuv run alembic upgrade head\nexport AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token\nuv run uvicorn app.main:app --reload --port 8010\n\n# Agents (separate terminals)\ncd agents\nuv sync\nexport GOOGLE_API_KEY=your_key\nexport AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token\nuv run adk api_server --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8021 .\n\n# Agents proxy (separate terminal)\ncd agents\nexport AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN=dev-internal-token\nuv run uvicorn app.proxy:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8020\n\n# Optional: ADK Web UI (separate terminal)\ncd agents\nuv run adk web --port 8001\n\n# Frontend (separate terminal)\ncd client\npnpm install\npnpm run dev\n```\n\nApp runs at http://localhost:5173 with API proxied to :8010.\n\n## Quality Gates\n\nBackend (from `server/`):\n```bash\nuv run ruff format --check .    # Formatting\nuv run ruff check .             # Linting\nuv run mypy .                   # Type checking (strict)\nuv run lint-imports             # Module boundaries\nuv run pytest tests/unit -q     # Unit tests\nuv run pytest tests/integration -q  # Integration tests\n```\n\nFrontend (from `client/`):\n```bash\npnpm run lint   # ESLint\npnpm run build  # TypeScript + Vite build\n```\n\nFrontend E2E (from `client/`):\n```bash\npnpm exec playwright install\npnpm run test:e2e\n```\n\n## API Endpoints\n\n| Method | Path | Auth | Description |\n|--------|------|------|-------------|\n| GET | `/health` | No | Health check |\n| POST | `/api/auth/signup` | No | Create account + set JWT cookie |\n| POST | `/api/auth/login` | No | Login + set JWT cookie |\n| POST | `/api/auth/logout` | Yes | Clear JWT cookie |\n| GET | `/api/auth/me` | Yes | Current user |\n| POST | `/api/chat` | Yes | Chat request |\n\n## Environment Variables\n\n| Variable | Default | Description |\n|----------|---------|-------------|\n| `ENV` | `development` | development / test / production |\n| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgresql+asyncpg://postgres:postgres@localhost:5433/app_scaffold` | Database connection |\n| `JWT_SECRET` | `app-scaffold-dev-secret` | JWT signing secret |\n| `JWT_ALGORITHM` | `HS256` | JWT algorithm |\n| `JWT_EXPIRES_MINUTES` | `60` | JWT expiration in minutes |\n| `AUTH_COOKIE_NAME` | `metrosense_token` | Auth cookie name |\n| `CORS_ORIGINS` | `http://localhost:5173,http://127.0.0.1:5173` | Allowed origins for cookies |\n\n## Docker\n\n```bash\n# Development (3 services)\ndocker-compose up\n\n# Production (single image)\ndocker build -t metrosense .\n```\n\n## Coding Style\n\n- Python: 4-space indent, type hints, `snake_case` functions, `PascalCase` classes\n- TypeScript: strict mode, `PascalCase` components, `camelCase` utilities\n- Backend layering: Routes → Services → DB (enforced)\n- Conventional Commits: `feat:`, `fix:`, `refactor:`\n"
  }
}
```

## Entry 67
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:12:16.156Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:12:16.156Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "exec_command",
    "arguments": "{\"cmd\":\"cd /home/dell/ai-track-metrosense && python3 - <<'PY'\\nimport csv, glob, os\\nfrom collections import Counter\\nfor path in sorted(glob.glob('server/DataSet_MetroSense/*.csv')):\\n    print('\\\\n##', os.path.basename(path))\\n    with open(path, newline='') as f:\\n        r = csv.DictReader(f)\\n        cols = r.fieldnames or []\\n        print('cols', len(cols))\\n        n=0\\n        null_counts=Counter()\\n        uniq_counts={c:set() for c in cols[:4]}\\n        for row in r:\\n            n+=1\\n            for c,v in row.items():\\n                if v=='' or v is None:\\n                    null_counts[c]+=1\\n            for c in cols[:4]:\\n                if len(uniq_counts[c])<5:\\n                    uniq_counts[c].add(row[c])\\n        print('rows', n)\\n        top_null=[(c,null_counts[c]) for c in cols if null_counts[c]]\\n        top_null=sorted(top_null,key=lambda x:-x[1])[:8]\\n        print('null_cols', len([1 for c in cols if null_counts[c]]), 'top', top_null)\\n        print('first_cols_sample_values', {k:list(v)[:3] for k,v in uniq_counts.items()})\\nPY\",\"max_output_tokens\":4000}",
    "call_id": "call_7qkU1PAdKh4OQRNvzF41MhWT"
  }
}
```

## Entry 71
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:12:34.256Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:12:34.256Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "exec_command",
    "arguments": "{\"cmd\":\"cd /home/dell/ai-track-metrosense && python3 - <<'PY'\\nimport csv,glob,os\\nfrom collections import Counter\\nchecks={\\n 'weather_observations.csv':['station_type','source_name','data_quality_flag','location_id','ward_id'],\\n 'air_quality_observations.csv':['station_type','source_name','aqi_category','dominant_pollutant','data_quality_flag','location_id','ward_id'],\\n 'lake_hydrology.csv':['source_name','overflow_status','gate_status','alert_level','encroachment_risk_flag','siltation_risk_flag','data_quality_flag','lake_id'],\\n 'power_outage_events.csv':['zone_name','outage_type','fault_type','source_name','data_quality_flag','location_id','ward_id'],\\n 'traffic_segment_observations.csv':['corridor_name','road_class','waterlogging_flag','incident_type','incident_severity','source_name','data_quality_flag','location_id','ward_id'],\\n 'flood_incidents.csv':['source_type','severity','data_quality_flag','location_id','ward_id','neighborhood_name']\\n}\\nfor path in sorted(glob.glob('server/DataSet_MetroSense/*.csv')):\\n    name=os.path.basename(path)\\n    cols=checks.get(name,[])\\n    print('\\\\n##',name)\\n    counters={c:Counter() for c in cols}\\n    with open(path,newline='') as f:\\n        r=csv.DictReader(f)\\n        for row in r:\\n            for c in cols:\\n                counters[c][row[c]]+=1\\n    for c in cols:\\n        vals=list(counters[c].items())\\n        print(c, 'unique=',len(vals), 'sample=', vals[:8])\\nPY\",\"max_output_tokens\":5000}",
    "call_id": "call_aq1xkQe7nA3CNNdarF7GcAyR"
  }
}
```

```json
    "output": "Chunk ID: 91b2b4\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 767\nOutput:\nfrom __future__ import annotations\n\nfrom collections.abc import AsyncGenerator\nimport os\nimport socket\nimport threading\nimport time\n\nimport pytest\nfrom fastapi import FastAPI\nfrom httpx import ASGITransport, AsyncClient\nimport uvicorn\nfrom sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import (\n    AsyncSession,\n    async_sessionmaker,\n    create_async_engine,\n)\n\nimport app.db.models  # noqa: F401 — register all models\nfrom app.api.deps import db_session as db_session_dep\nfrom app.api.router import root_router\nfrom app.core.config import get_settings\nfrom app.db.base import Base\nfrom app.middleware.error_handler import ErrorHandlerMiddleware\nfrom app.middleware.request_logging import RequestLoggingMiddleware\nfrom tests.integration.agent_stub import app as agent_stub_app\n\nAGENT_HOST = \"127.0.0.1\"\nAGENT_PORT = 18020\n\n\ndef _create_test_app() -> FastAPI:\n    \"\"\"Create a FastAPI app without lifespan (no shared engine).\"\"\"\n    test_app = FastAPI(title=\"Test\")\n    test_app.add_middleware(RequestLoggingMiddleware)\n    test_app.add_middleware(ErrorHandlerMiddleware)\n    test_app.include_router(root_router)\n    return test_app\n\n\n@pytest.fixture(scope=\"session\", autouse=True)\ndef agent_server() -> None:\n    os.environ[\"AGENT_SERVER_URL\"] = f\"http://{AGENT_HOST}:{AGENT_PORT}\"\n    os.environ[\"AGENT_INTERNAL_TOKEN\"] = \"test-internal-token\"\n    os.environ[\"JWT_SECRET\"] = \"test-secret-at-least-32-bytes-long\"\n    get_settings.cache_clear()\n\n    config = uvicorn.Config(agent_stub_app, host=AGENT_HOST, port=AGENT_PORT, log_level=\"warning\")\n    server = uvicorn.Server(config=config)\n    thread = threading.Thread(target=server.run, daemon=True)\n    thread.start()\n\n    for _ in range(50):\n        with socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM) as sock:\n            if sock.connect_ex((AGENT_HOST, AGENT_PORT)) == 0:\n                break\n        time.sleep(0.1)\n\n    yield\n    server.should_exit = True\n    thread.join(timeout=5)\n\n\n@pytest.fixture\nasync def engine():  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]\n    settings = get_settings()\n    eng = create_async_engine(settings.effective_db_url, echo=False)\n    async with eng.begin() as conn:\n        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.create_all)\n    yield eng\n    async with eng.begin() as conn:\n        await conn.run_sync(Base.metadata.drop_all)\n    await eng.dispose()\n\n\n@pytest.fixture\nasync def session_factory(engine):  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]\n    return async_sessionmaker(engine, expire_on_commit=False)\n\n\n@pytest.fixture\nasync def db(session_factory):  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]\n    async with session_factory() as session:\n        yield session\n\n\n@pytest.fixture\nasync def client(session_factory):  # type: ignore[no-untyped-def]\n    test_app = _create_test_app()\n\n    async def _override() -> AsyncGenerator[AsyncSession, None]:\n        async with session_factory() as session:\n            yield session\n\n    test_app.dependency_overrides[db_session_dep] = _override\n    transport = ASGITransport(app=test_app)\n    async with AsyncClient(transport=transport, base_url=\"http://test\") as ac:\n        yield ac\n"
  }
}
```

```json
{
```

```json
    "output": "Chunk ID: 1a71b5\nWall time: 0.0001 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 4611\nOutput:\n## server/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_index.json\n{\n  \"document_id\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022\",\n  \"title\": \"Urban Flood Vulnerability and Inundation Dynamics in Bangalore — A Technical Study\",\n  \"agency\": \"Indian Institute of Science (IISc) — Centre for Infrastructure, Sustainable Transportation and Urban Planning (CiSTUP)\",\n  \"document_type\": \"flood_research\",\n  \"published_at\": \"2022-11\",\n  \"wards_covered\": [\n    \"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\n    \"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Whitefield\",\"HSR Layout\"\n  ],\n  \"source_path\": \"/documents/raw/bangalore_flood_study_2022.pdf\",\n  \"total_pages\": 112,\n  \"total_sections\": 5,\n  \"summary\": \"Technical research study examining urban flood vulnerability in Bangalore with empirical analysis of flood events from 2015–2022. Establishes rainfall-inundation correlation curves for major wards, analyses lake backflow contribution to road inundation, provides corridor-level delay multipliers for logistics planning, and recommends ward-level early warning thresholds. Calibration reference for the UrbanClimate AI flood and logistics agents.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Study Scope, Methodology, and Ward Coverage\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 18,\n      \"summary\": \"Overview of study design, data sources used, spatial coverage, and field survey methodology. Covers 10 high-risk wards with documented flood history from 2015–2022. Establishes the analytical framework used in subsequent sections.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Whitefield\",\"HSR Layout\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"Rainfall-Inundation Correlation Findings by Ward\",\n      \"page_start\": 19, \"page_end\": 48,\n      \"summary\": \"Core empirical findings — rainfall intensity and duration thresholds that produce measurable road inundation in each study ward. Includes antecedent soil moisture correction factors and prior-day rainfall cumulative effects on inundation probability.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Lake Backflow Contribution Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 49, \"page_end\": 72,\n      \"summary\": \"Quantitative analysis of how lake fill levels amplify surface flood risk. Isolates lake backflow contribution from direct rainfall contribution for Bellandur, Varthur, and KR Puram lake systems. Provides compound probability curves for joint lake-fill and rainfall events.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s3.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s4\",\n      \"title\": \"Road Corridor Vulnerability Rankings and Delay Multipliers\",\n      \"page_start\": 73, \"page_end\": 94,\n      \"summary\": \"Empirical delay multipliers for six major Bangalore road corridors as a function of rainfall intensity band. Based on GPS probe vehicle data and incident records. Provides logistics planning reference for estimating travel time degradation under rain conditions.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Silk Board\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s5\",\n      \"title\": \"Early Warning Thresholds and Recommendations\",\n      \"page_start\": 95, \"page_end\": 112,\n      \"summary\": \"Research-derived early warning threshold recommendations for each study ward. Covers lead time requirements, recommended monitoring parameters, and threshold values calibrated against observed flood events. Intended for integration into BBMP disaster management protocols.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Varthur\",\"Sarjapur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"Hebbal\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bangalore_flood_study_2022_s5.md\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n\n## server/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_index.json\n{\n  \"document_id\": \"bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024\",\n  \"title\": \"BBMP Tree Canopy and Treefall Risk Reference — Bengaluru\",\n  \"agency\": \"Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP) — Horticulture Department\",\n  \"document_type\": \"tree_density_map\",\n  \"published_at\": \"2024-11\",\n  \"wards_covered\": [\n    \"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\n    \"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\n    \"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\n    \"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"\n  ],\n  \"source_path\": \"/documents/raw/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024.pdf\",\n  \"total_pages\": 64,\n  \"total_sections\": 4,\n  \"summary\": \"BBMP tree census-derived reference document covering zone-level canopy density, ward-level tree risk ratings, fall-prone species identification, and high-risk treefall corridors mapped to road segments. Based on BBMP Tree Census data from November 2024. Primary reference for the Infrastructure Stress agent when assessing treefall risk and powerline proximity hazard.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Zone-Level Canopy Density Summary\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 16,\n      \"summary\": \"Zone-wise tree density and species composition across all 8 BBMP zones. Derived from the November 2024 tree census (approximately 592,000 trees enumerated). Covers total tree count, dominant species by zone, average tree age, and canopy coverage percentage per zone.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"Ward-Level Canopy Ratings and Powerline Proximity Risk\",\n      \"page_start\": 17, \"page_end\": 36,\n      \"summary\": \"Ward-level assessment of tree canopy density and proximity to BESCOM overhead distribution lines. Each ward rated on canopy density, species mix, overhead line exposure, and resulting treefall-powerline contact risk. Covers all 16 wards.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Fall-Prone Species Identification and Risk Profiles\",\n      \"page_start\": 37, \"page_end\": 50,\n      \"summary\": \"Detailed risk profiles for the three primary fall-prone species in Bangalore — Rain Tree, Silver Oak, and Eucalyptus. Covers physical characteristics, failure modes, wind speed at which failure probability exceeds key thresholds, and geographic concentration by ward.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Koramangala\",\"Jayanagar\",\"Bellandur\",\"Hebbal\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Peenya\",\"Yelahanka\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s4\",\n      \"title\": \"High-Risk Treefall Corridors Mapped to Road Segments\",\n      \"page_start\": 51, \"page_end\": 64,\n      \"summary\": \"Specific road segment level identification of high-risk treefall corridors where tree canopy, powerline proximity, and historical incident data combine to produce elevated treefall-outage risk. Provides actionable reference for infrastructure risk scoring and pre-storm crew positioning.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Jayanagar\",\"Hebbal\",\"Whitefield\",\"Rajajinagar\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s4.md\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n\n## server/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json\n{\n  \"document_id\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023\",\n  \"title\": \"BESCOM Infrastructure and Storm Incident Reference — Bengaluru Distribution Network\",\n  \"agency\": \"Bangalore Electricity Supply Company (BESCOM)\",\n  \"document_type\": \"bescom_report\",\n  \"published_at\": \"2023-03\",\n  \"wards_covered\": [\n    \"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\n    \"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\n    \"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\n    \"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"\n  ],\n  \"source_path\": \"/documents/raw/bescom_infra_reference_2023.pdf\",\n  \"total_pages\": 89,\n  \"total_sections\": 5,\n  \"summary\": \"BESCOM distribution network reference covering HT/LT line inventory, historical storm-related outage analysis, feeder-level incident records, ward-level outage risk ratings, and storm response protocols. Primary reference for the Infrastructure Stress agent when reasoning about treefall-outage risk and feeder vulnerability during wind and rain events.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Network Overview — HT/LT Lines, Substations, and Feeder Inventory\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 18,\n      \"summary\": \"Complete inventory of BESCOM distribution network within BBMP limits. Covers HT and LT line totals, substation count and capacity, feeder count by zone, and network growth trends from 2018 to 2022. Establishes network scale context for risk analysis in subsequent sections.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"Treefall-Outage Correlation Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 19, \"page_end\": 38,\n      \"summary\": \"Analysis of historical treefall-caused outage events from 2018–2022. Establishes wind gust thresholds at which treefall probability increases significantly for each dominant tree species. Identifies high-risk zone and species combinations. Primary reference for treefall risk scoring.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Koramangala\",\"Jayanagar\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Whitefield\",\"Bellandur\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Feeder-Level Incident History — Storm Events 2018–2022\",\n      \"page_start\": 39, \"page_end\": 58,\n      \"summary\": \"Detailed incident record for each of the five primary monitored feeders. Covers total outage count, fault type distribution, wind conditions at time of event, affected customers, and average restoration time. Calibration data for feeder-level risk scoring.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Whitefield\",\"Hebbal\",\"Peenya\",\"KR Puram\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s4\",\n      \"title\": \"Ward-Level Outage Risk Ratings\",\n      \"page_start\": 59, \"page_end\": 74,\n      \"summary\": \"Outage risk assessment for all 16 wards. Each ward rated on feeder exposure, overhead line density, proximity to high-canopy zones, historical incident rate, and critical load sensitivity. Provides overall outage risk tier and primary risk driver per ward.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023_s4.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s5\",\n      \"title\": \"Storm Response Protocols and Restoration Time Benchmarks\",\n      \"page_start\": 75, \"page_end\": 89,\n      \"summary\": \"BESCOM operational protocols for storm response including wind gust alert thresholds, crew pre-positioning guidelines, and restoration time benchmarks by fault type. Reference for estimating outage duration and customer impact in risk assessments.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"bescom_infra_reference_2023_s5.md\"\n    }\n  ]\n}\n\n## server/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_index.json\n{\n  \"document_id\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019\",\n  \"title\": \"BBMP Stormwater Drainage Master Plan — Bengaluru\",\n  \"agency\": \"Bruhat Bengaluru Mahanagara Palike (BBMP)\",\n  \"document_type\": \"drainage_plan\",\n  \"published_at\": \"2019-06\",\n  \"wards_covered\": [\n    \"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\n    \"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"HSR Layout\",\"KR Puram\",\n    \"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\",\n    \"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Yeshwanthpur\",\n    \"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"\n  ],\n  \"source_path\": \"/documents/raw/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019.pdf\",\n  \"total_pages\": 198,\n  \"total_sections\": 6,\n  \"summary\": \"Comprehensive stormwater drainage master plan covering all BBMP ward boundaries. Documents existing drainage network capacity, identifies critical underpass and culvert choke points, establishes rainfall intensity thresholds for barricade deployment, and provides ward-level drainage capacity ratings. Primary reference for flood vulnerability assessment and underpass risk scoring.\",\n  \"table_of_contents\": [\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s1\",\n      \"title\": \"Overview and Scope of Bangalore Drainage Network\",\n      \"page_start\": 1, \"page_end\": 18,\n      \"summary\": \"Background on Bangalore stormwater drainage network — total channel length, primary and secondary drain classifications, lake interconnection structure, and coverage gaps.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": false,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": false,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": false,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s2\",\n      \"title\": \"ORR Corridor Culvert and Underpass Capacity Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 42, \"page_end\": 67,\n      \"summary\": \"Hydraulic capacity analysis of all major underpasses and culverts on the Outer Ring Road corridor. Includes design discharge values, observed failure thresholds, and barricade deployment trigger points.\",\n      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\"],\n      \"contains_thresholds\": true,\n      \"contains_recommendations\": true,\n      \"contains_capacity_data\": true,\n      \"text_file\": \"drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s2.md\"\n    },\n    {\n      \"section_id\": \"s3\",\n      \"title\": \"Sarjapur Road and Varthur Catchment Drainage Analysis\",\n      \"page_start\": 68, \"page_end\": 94,\n      \"summary\": \"Catchment analysis for Sarjapur Road corridor and Varthur lake sub-basin.     "output": "Chunk ID: 449b18\nWall time: 0.0000 seconds\nProcess exited with code 0\nOriginal token count: 4262\nOutput:\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json:8:    \"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json:9:    \"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json:34:      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Koramangala\",\"Jayanagar\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Whitefield\",\"Bellandur\"],\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json:45:      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Whitefield\",\"Hebbal\",\"Peenya\",\"KR Puram\"],\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_index.json:56:      \"wards_mentioned\": [\"Bellandur\",\"Koramangala\",\"Silk Board\",\"BTM Layout\",\"Sarjapur\",\"Varthur\",\"KR Puram\",\"Hebbal\",\"Yelahanka\",\"Manyata Tech Park\",\"Whitefield\",\"Peenya\",\"Rajajinagar\",\"Yeshwanthpur\",\"Jayanagar\",\"MG Road\"],\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s5.md:27:- **Emergency crews pre-positioned** at high-risk wards: Bellandur, Hebbal, Koramangala, Jayanagar\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s5.md:62:When 3 or more faults on the same feeder or sub-zone occur within a 90-minute window, restoration time escalates non-linearly because crews must sequentially clear each fault before energising — the feeder cannot be restored with any fault remaining. September 2022 Bellandur event (3 simultaneous treefall faults) resulted in 6.5 hours total restoration time due to this sequential requirement.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s5.md:90:**In flood conditions:** If the feeder route passes through a flooded area, energisation is held until water has subsided and a physical inspection confirms no cable submersion. This protocol is specifically relevant for Bellandur, KR Puram, and Varthur wards where flood and outage events frequently coincide.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s5.md:104:Wind gust telemetry from BESCOM's 12 field weather stations (located at major substations) is shared with IMD and KSNDMC for joint situational awareness during storm events. Station locations include: Bellandur SS, Whitefield SS, Hebbal SS, Peenya SS, KR Puram SS, Yeshwanthpur SS, Jayanagar SS, Koramangala SS, BTM SS, Manyata SS, Yelahanka SS, Silk Board SS.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s1.md:22:**BBMP Zones covered:** Bommanahalli (includes Bellandur, Koramangala, Silk Board, HSR Layout, BTM Layout, Sarjapur, Varthur)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s1.md:52:**BBMP Zones covered:** Yelahanka (includes Yelahanka, Hebbal, Manyata Tech Park, Thanisandra, Nagavara)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s1.md:62:**Zone risk profile:** Highest canopy coverage in BBMP — Yelahanka, Hebbal, and the Manyata corridor retain significant green cover compared to south Bangalore. Bellary Road is particularly notable for its dense Rain Tree median and roadside planting extending from Hebbal to the airport boundary.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:14:## 3.2 Bellandur Feeder A (fdr_001)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:16:**Substation:** Bellandur 33/11 kV Substation (sub_001)  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:17:**Ward:** Bellandur  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:21:**Critical load flag:** YES — feeds Bellandur ward BBMP office, two private hospitals, and ESF data centre  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:41:- ORR service road between Bellandur signal and Agara Junction (11 incidents) — dense Rain Tree canopy, overhead 11 kV lines on both sides\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:42:- Bellandur Main Road near State Bank Colony (8 incidents)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:43:- Sarjapur Road frontage near Bellandur lake bund road (7 incidents)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:75:**Key characteristics:** Higher conductor failure rate (28%) compared to Bellandur Feeder A (19%), reflecting older conductor stock in parts of the Whitefield network built out during the early 2000s IT boom with lower specification materials. Treefall risk moderate due to mixed canopy type.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:79:## 3.4 Hebbal Feeder A (fdr_003)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:81:**Substation:** Hebbal 33/11 kV Substation (sub_003)  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:82:**Ward:** Hebbal  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:86:**Critical load flag:** YES — feeds Hebbal ward BBMP facility and two government hospitals  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:99:**Notable characteristic:** Zero transformer faults in the 5-year period — Hebbal substation transformer was replaced in 2017 and is among the newest transformer stock in north Bangalore. High lightning fault share (18%) reflects north Bangalore's exposure to pre-monsoon and early monsoon convective storms that track from the north-west.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:102:The 3.2 km overhead section along Bellary Road (Hebbal to Nagavara junction) is the highest-risk stretch on this feeder — 19 of 27 treefall faults originated in this stretch. Rain Tree density along Bellary Road median and roadside plantings is very high.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s3.md:150:**Key characteristic:** Second highest total unplanned outages after Bellandur Feeder A, with high treefall share (62%) and highest overhead line ratio (71%). The eastern Whitefield Road section of this feeder runs through a mixed-canopy residential corridor with significant Rain Tree planting dating from the 1990s BDA layout development.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md:26:- Hebbal Valley drain — 29.6 km, outfalls north toward Yelahanka\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md:27:- Nagavara drain — 18.2 km, feeds Hebbal lake system\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md:47:When lake fill levels exceed 85%, the retention function is effectively saturated. Rainfall events occurring when lakes are at or above 85% capacity result in rapid overland flow because the lake system can no longer absorb surges. This is the primary mechanism behind flash flooding in Bellandur, Varthur, and KR Puram wards during late monsoon season (August–October), when cumulative fill from earlier rains leaves lakes with minimal buffer capacity.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s1.md:74:- **Section 4:** North Bangalore — Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Tech Park corridor\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s2.md:46:- Bellary Road (Hebbal to Yelahanka) — Road median planting near Manyata\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s2.md:120:| ORR – Bellandur to Koramangala | Bellandur, Koramangala | Rain Tree | HIGH | 4.2 events/km/yr |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s2.md:122:| Bellary Road – Hebbal to Nagavara | Hebbal | Rain Tree | HIGH | 3.6 events/km/yr |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md:29:| Bellandur | 47 | Lake backflow + surface runoff | **Primary** |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md:36:| Hebbal | 22 | Lake backflow cascade (Nagavara → Hebbal) | Secondary |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md:48:KSNDMC telemetry from 6 primary lakes (Bellandur, Varthur, Hebbal, Nagavara, KR Puram, Madiwala). 15-minute interval readings. Fill percentage calculated against Full Tank Level (FTL) for each lake.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md:72:Both mechanisms operating simultaneously. Produces the deepest and most persistent flooding. September 2022 events in Bellandur and Varthur were predominantly compound events.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s1.md:74:Most previous threshold analyses treated all Bangalore flooding as pluvial. A key finding of this study is that **32 of the 47 Bellandur incidents and 29 of the 38 Varthur incidents involved a significant fluvial backflow component** — meaning rainfall-only thresholds systematically underestimate risk in lake-adjacent wards.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:40:When Bellandur lake ≥ 85%, add 0.3 to all multipliers above 1.4 due to increased probability of Bellandur ORR and Agara underpass closures creating route diversions that flood the ORR surface road.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:89:## 4.5 Bellary Road — Hebbal to Yelahanka\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:101:| 15–25 mm/hr | 1.5–1.9 | Hebbal underpass approaches begin congesting |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:102:| 25–40 mm/hr | 2.0–2.5 | Hebbal flyover underpass closure creates major diversion |\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:105:**Hebbal lake supplemental multiplier:**  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bangalore_flood_study_2022_s4.md:106:When Hebbal lake ≥ 85%, add 0.4 to multipliers above 1.4. Airport cargo and scheduled logistics operators should note that Bellary Road is the only direct route to KIAL — no practical alternative route exists for HGV cargo.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:11:The Varthur lake sub-basin is the largest catchment in south-east Bangalore, covering approximately 224 km². The sub-basin drains via the Koramangala–Chellaghatta (KC) Valley primary drain, which originates near Koramangala and flows east through Bellandur, Varthur, and exits BBMP limits toward Varthur Kodi village.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:17:- Number of interconnected lakes in cascade: 7 (Madiwala → Bellandur → Varthur in the main chain)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:20:The Varthur lake itself is the terminal lake in the KC Valley chain. It receives inflow from Bellandur lake through the connecting channel (Bellandur–Varthur kaluve) in addition to direct catchment runoff. When both Bellandur and Varthur lakes approach capacity simultaneously — which occurs during sustained monsoon periods — the connecting channel becomes bidirectional and inundation risk on Sarjapur Road escalates sharply.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:90:The western portion of the Varthur catchment — HSR Layout and south Koramangala — discharges into the KC Valley drain via three secondary channels. During peak monsoon, the combined discharge from these channels represents approximately 38% of total KC Valley flow at the Bellandur lake inlet.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:94:- HSR Layout Sector 1 and 2 (lower elevation, near Agara lake) are affected by backwater when Bellandur lake exceeds 85%, slowing drain outflow and causing localised waterlogging in storm drains\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:33:**South Zone** (Bellandur, Koramangala, Silk Board, BTM Layout, Sarjapur, Varthur, HSR Layout)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:47:**North Zone** (Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Tech Park, Nagavara, Thanisandra)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:52:- Treefall risk: **MODERATE** — good canopy density but wind exposure higher (open terrain north of Hebbal)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:53:- ORR between Bellandur and KR Puram — service road double row planting (8,400 trees)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:55:- Bellary Road, Hebbal to Yelahanka — median and roadside (920 trees in 6.2 km)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:136:- GKVK campus and Hebbal periphery — 2,100 trees\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:1:# Section 4 — North Bangalore Drainage: Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Corridor\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:5:**Wards:** Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Tech Park  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:11:North Bangalore drains via the Hebbal Valley, which flows generally north-east from the Sankey Tank area through Hebbal lake and outfalls toward Yelahanka and ultimately into the Arkavathi river sub-basin. Unlike the Varthur catchment, north Bangalore benefits from lower overall impervious cover in its upper reaches — Yelahanka and peripheral areas retain significant green cover — but faces a concentrated risk zone around the Hebbal flyover and the Nagavara–Hebbal lake interconnection.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:14:- Total Hebbal Valley catchment area: 118 km²\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:17:- Lakes in cascade: Sankey Tank → Nagavara Lake → Hebbal Lake → Yelahanka tank system\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:20:The critical vulnerability in this corridor is the **Hebbal flyover underpass and the Nagavara–Hebbal connecting channel** — a 2.4 km channel that becomes the primary flood pathway when Nagavara lake fill exceeds 80%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:24:## 4.2 Hebbal Lake and Nagavara Lake Interaction\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:26:Hebbal lake (max capacity: 1.4 MCM) receives inflow from two sources: direct surface runoff from its 22 km² immediate catchment, and overflow from Nagavara lake through the connecting channel when Nagavara fill exceeds approximately 78%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:29:When Nagavara lake fill exceeds **82%**, the connecting channel flow rate increases sharply from its normal 0.8–1.2 m³/s baseflow to 6–9 m³/s, effectively doubling Hebbal lake inflow rate within 90 minutes. Under concurrent rainfall conditions, Hebbal lake fill can rise from 60% to 92% within a single 6-hour heavy rain event.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:32:- 0600h: Nagavara fill 79%, Hebbal fill 58%, rainfall beginning\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:33:- 0900h: Nagavara fill 88%, Hebbal fill 74%, connecting channel running at 7.4 m³/s\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:34:- 1200h: Hebbal fill 91%, Hebbal lake alert level DANGER, Bellary Road underpass flooded\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:35:- 1400h: Hebbal flyover underpass fully closed, water depth 0.8m\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:41:### 4.3.1 Hebbal Flyover Underpass (Bellary Road km 8.1)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:43:**Location:** Bellary Road below Hebbal flyover, approximately 600m south of Hebbal lake bund  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:51:The Hebbal flyover underpass fails through a combination of surface runoff accumulation and seepage/overflow from Hebbal lake bund. The lake bund's southern face is 310m from the underpass. When lake fill exceeds **88%**, seepage through the bund toe reaches the underpass drainage pit via subsurface flow, supplementing surface runoff and accelerating pit fill rate by approximately 35%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:55:- Hebbal lake fill ≥ 85% AND rainfall ≥ 20 mm/hr, OR\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:59:**Recommended barricade position:** Bellary Road 300m north and south of underpass; Hebbal Junction approach roads\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:65:**Location:** Bellary Road at Esteem Mall signal, Hebbal ward  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:71:- Hebbal lake fill ≥ 90%\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:79:Manyata Tech Park occupies approximately 4.8 km² in Nagavara ward, immediately north-east of Hebbal lake. The development was constructed on former tank bed and low-lying land. Despite internal SWD infrastructure built to higher design standards, Manyata faces drainage risk through two external pathways:\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:94:Yelahanka ward sits at the upper end of the Hebbal Valley drainage system. Its elevation advantage (average 915–920m MSL versus Hebbal at 905–908m MSL) means it does not experience backwater flooding from Hebbal lake. However, two localised waterlogging zones exist:\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s6.md:25:### Bellandur\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s6.md:32:- **Notes:** Lake backflow from Bellandur lake compounds drainage deficit during late monsoon. No improvement in drainage capacity since 2012 despite 40% increase in built-up area.\n"
  }
}
```

It receives inflow from Bellandur lake through the connecting channel (Bellandur–Varthur kaluve) in addition to direct catchment runoff. When both Bellandur and Varthur lakes approach capacity simultaneously — which occurs during sustained monsoon periods — the connecting channel becomes bidirectional and inundation risk on Sarjapur Road escalates sharply.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:90:The western portion of the Varthur catchment — HSR Layout and south Koramangala — discharges into the KC Valley drain via three secondary channels. During peak monsoon, the combined discharge from these channels represents approximately 38% of total KC Valley flow at the Bellandur lake inlet.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s3.md:94:- HSR Layout Sector 1 and 2 (lower elevation, near Agara lake) are affected by backwater when Bellandur lake exceeds 85%, slowing drain outflow and causing localised waterlogging in storm drains\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:33:**South Zone** (Bellandur, Koramangala, Silk Board, BTM Layout, Sarjapur, Varthur, HSR Layout)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:47:**North Zone** (Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Tech Park, Nagavara, Thanisandra)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bescom_infra_reference_2023_s1.md:52:- Treefall risk: **MODERATE** — good canopy density but wind exposure higher (open terrain north of Hebbal)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:53:- ORR between Bellandur and KR Puram — service road double row planting (8,400 trees)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:55:- Bellary Road, Hebbal to Yelahanka — median and roadside (920 trees in 6.2 km)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/bbmp_tree_canopy_reference_2024_s3.md:136:- GKVK campus and Hebbal periphery — 2,100 trees\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:1:# Section 4 — North Bangalore Drainage: Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Corridor\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:5:**Wards:** Hebbal, Yelahanka, Manyata Tech Park  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:11:North Bangalore drains via the Hebbal Valley, which flows generally north-east from the Sankey Tank area through Hebbal lake and outfalls toward Yelahanka and ultimately into the Arkavathi river sub-basin. Unlike the Varthur catchment, north Bangalore benefits from lower overall impervious cover in its upper reaches — Yelahanka and peripheral areas retain significant green cover — but faces a concentrated risk zone around the Hebbal flyover and the Nagavara–Hebbal lake interconnection.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:14:- Total Hebbal Valley catchment area: 118 km²\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:17:- Lakes in cascade: Sankey Tank → Nagavara Lake → Hebbal Lake → Yelahanka tank system\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:20:The critical vulnerability in this corridor is the **Hebbal flyover underpass and the Nagavara–Hebbal connecting channel** — a 2.4 km channel that becomes the primary flood pathway when Nagavara lake fill exceeds 80%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:24:## 4.2 Hebbal Lake and Nagavara Lake Interaction\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:26:Hebbal lake (max capacity: 1.4 MCM) receives inflow from two sources: direct surface runoff from its 22 km² immediate catchment, and overflow from Nagavara lake through the connecting channel when Nagavara fill exceeds approximately 78%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:29:When Nagavara lake fill exceeds **82%**, the connecting channel flow rate increases sharply from its normal 0.8–1.2 m³/s baseflow to 6–9 m³/s, effectively doubling Hebbal lake inflow rate within 90 minutes. Under concurrent rainfall conditions, Hebbal lake fill can rise from 60% to 92% within a single 6-hour heavy rain event.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:32:- 0600h: Nagavara fill 79%, Hebbal fill 58%, rainfall beginning\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:33:- 0900h: Nagavara fill 88%, Hebbal fill 74%, connecting channel running at 7.4 m³/s\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:34:- 1200h: Hebbal fill 91%, Hebbal lake alert level DANGER, Bellary Road underpass flooded\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:35:- 1400h: Hebbal flyover underpass fully closed, water depth 0.8m\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:41:### 4.3.1 Hebbal Flyover Underpass (Bellary Road km 8.1)\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:43:**Location:** Bellary Road below Hebbal flyover, approximately 600m south of Hebbal lake bund  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:51:The Hebbal flyover underpass fails through a combination of surface runoff accumulation and seepage/overflow from Hebbal lake bund. The lake bund's southern face is 310m from the underpass. When lake fill exceeds **88%**, seepage through the bund toe reaches the underpass drainage pit via subsurface flow, supplementing surface runoff and accelerating pit fill rate by approximately 35%.\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:55:- Hebbal lake fill ≥ 85% AND rainfall ≥ 20 mm/hr, OR\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:59:**Recommended barricade position:** Bellary Road 300m north and south of underpass; Hebbal Junction approach roads\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:65:**Location:** Bellary Road at Esteem Mall signal, Hebbal ward  \nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:71:- Hebbal lake fill ≥ 90%\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:79:Manyata Tech Park occupies approximately 4.8 km² in Nagavara ward, immediately north-east of Hebbal lake. The development was constructed on former tank bed and low-lying land. Despite internal SWD infrastructure built to higher design standards, Manyata faces drainage risk through two external pathways:\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s4.md:94:Yelahanka ward sits at the upper end of the Hebbal Valley drainage system. Its elevation advantage (average 915–920m MSL versus Hebbal at 905–908m MSL) means it does not experience backwater flooding from Hebbal lake. However, two localised waterlogging zones exist:\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s6.md:25:### Bellandur\nserver/Documents_Metrosense/drainage_plan_bbmp_2019_s6.md:32:- **Notes:** Lake backflow from Bellandur lake compounds drainage deficit during late monsoon. No improvement in drainage capacity since 2012 despite 40% increase in built-up area.\n"
```json
  }
}
```

## Entry 95
**Timestamp:** 2026-03-11T05:13:20.483Z
**Role/Type:** response_item
**Content:**
```json
{
  "timestamp": "2026-03-11T05:13:20.483Z",
  "type": "response_item",
  "payload": {
    "type": "function_call",
    "name": "request_user_input",
    "arguments": "{\"questions\":[{\"header\":\"Schema Scope\",\"id\":\"schema_scope\",\"question\":\"For this phase, how broad should the Postgres schema be relative to the architecture doc?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Core telemetry now (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Create tables directly needed for the CSVs plus required reference tables; defer non-dataset operational tables from the doc.\"},{\"label\":\"All 12 architecture tables\",\"description\":\"Implement the full normalized 12-table target now, including sessions/history/audit tables.\"},{\"label\":\"CSV tables only\",\"description\":\"Create only raw CSV-backed tables and skip/loosen reference normalization for now.\"}]},{\"header\":\"Reference Source\",\"id\":\"reference_source\",\"question\":\"How should we populate canonical reference entities (locations, wards, lakes) that are not fully explicit in one CSV?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Infer + normalize (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Build reference rows from CSV distinct values and enrich from document indexes where needed.\"},{\"label\":\"Strict CSV-only\",\"description\":\"Populate references only from CSV values exactly as-is; no document-derived enrichment.\"},{\"label\":\"Manual seed file\",\"description\":\"Add a hand-maintained reference seed file and use CSV only for telemetry/event tables.\"}]},{\"header\":\"Load Method\",\"id\":\"load_method\",\"question\":\"What loading workflow do you want for the golden CSV data?\",\"options\":[{\"label\":\"Idempotent loader command (Recommended)\",\"description\":\"Add a dedicated load script/command (truncate+load or upsert) run manually after migrations.\"},{\"label\":\"Alembic data migration\",\"description\":\"Load CSV data directly inside Alembic revisions.\"},{\"label\":\"App startup auto-load\",\"description\":\"Attempt to load/refresh golden data automatically when the backend starts.\"}]}]}",
    "call_id": "call_6YtiAajZFaHmTV3EFBFBgRsh"
  }
}
```
